agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature where three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided conflicting requirements about when an order may be cancelled. The PM allows cancellation before shipment, but Finance forbids cancellation of invoiced orders, and Operations invoices at placement — meaning orders are invoiced immediately and can never be cancelled under the Finance rule, directly contradicting the PM's requirement.","bounded_context":"Order Lifecycle Management — spanning order placement, invoicing, cancellation, and shipment.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through placement, invoicing, and shipment stages."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped. Contested: the allowable cancellation window is the core conflict."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document locking in the pricing of an order. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. PM considers this the cancellation cutoff."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order progresses through a lifecycle: Placed → Invoiced → Shipped.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"State transitions define the order lifecycle and are fundamental to system integrity. Without them, order tracking is meaningless.","testable_assertion":"Every order must have a recorded state that is one of: Placed, Invoiced, Shipped, or Cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Invoicing is irreversible — once an order is invoiced, the invoice cannot be undone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares this a hard financial constraint. Reversing an invoice has accounting, tax, and audit implications that make it a data integrity issue.","testable_assertion":"No operation in the system may transition an order from Invoiced back to a pre-invoiced state."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"An order that has been shipped cannot be cancelled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical shipment is irreversible — goods have left the warehouse. All three stakeholders implicitly agree on this boundary.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in Shipped state, any cancellation request must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"[CONTESTED] PM states: an order can be cancelled at any time before shipment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product policy, not a physical or financial constraint. It is the rule in direct conflict with BR-5.","testable_assertion":"Given an order not yet shipped, a cancellation request should succeed — BUT this contradicts BR-5."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"[CONTESTED] Finance states: an invoiced order cannot be cancelled. Operations states: orders are invoiced at placement. Combined effect: no order can ever be cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is the combined policy of Finance and Operations. If taken literally, cancellation is impossible since every placed order is immediately invoiced.","testable_assertion":"Given any placed order, it is already invoiced, therefore cancellation must be rejected — BUT this contradicts BR-4."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but NOT yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"[BLOCKED — contradictory rules] Either the cancellation succeeds (per PM) or is rejected (per Finance+Ops). Cannot implement until stakeholders resolve the conflict."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation is rejected. All stakeholders agree on this boundary."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An invoice has been created for an order","when":"Any operation attempts to reverse or void the invoice","then":"The operation is rejected — invoicing is irreversible per Finance."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order is being placed","when":"The order transitions to Placed state","then":"An invoice is immediately generated to lock in pricing, per Operations."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer attempts to cancel an order milliseconds after placement but after the invoice is generated.","expected_behavior":"Undefined — this is the exact collision point of the contradiction. PM says allow it; Finance says deny it."},{"scenario":"A future process change decouples invoicing from placement (e.g., invoice at shipment).","expected_behavior":"Would resolve the contradiction by creating a cancellation window between placement and invoicing. This is one possible resolution path."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not.","expected_behavior":"Not addressed in requirements. If cancellation of unshipped items is allowed, the invoicing constraint still applies to the entire order."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund issued against an invoice instead of reversing it.","expected_behavior":"Could be a resolution mechanism — Finance's 'irreversible' constraint on the invoice is preserved, but a compensating transaction (credit note) enables effective cancellation."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked entirely (Finance+Ops wins), customers have no recourse before shipment, leading to higher return rates, chargebacks, and customer support costs. If cancellation is allowed (PM wins), Finance must handle invoice reversals or credit notes, adding accounting complexity.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any cancellation mechanism must either work around irreversible invoices (via credit notes) or change the invoicing timing — both require process changes.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. Blocking all cancellations would severely damage customer experience and trust, likely increasing churn and negative reviews.","risk":"Implementing either interpretation without stakeholder alignment will result in one team's process being violated. Shipping without resolution risks financial audit issues (if invoices are effectively reversed) or customer backlash (if cancellation is impossible).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — cannot cancel orders if Finance+Ops interpretation prevails, leading to poor experience and potential regulatory issues (consumer protection laws often mandate cancellation rights)."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must handle accounting implications of whichever resolution is chosen. Credit notes add reconciliation overhead; changing invoice timing affects pricing lock-in."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change when invoicing occurs, or support a new credit note workflow for cancellations."},{"group":"Product/PM","impact":"Core feature requirement is blocked until contradiction is resolved."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is impossible, or handle edge cases in whatever hybrid solution is adopted."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements are mutually incompatible. The PM's cancellation window (before shipment) is entirely consumed by Operations' immediate invoicing, which Finance says blocks cancellation. The spec as written makes the cancellation feature logically impossible to implement. Confidence is low because no correct implementation can exist without stakeholder resolution.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — not a matter of interpretation","Invoicing irreversibility is a hard financial constraint","All parties agree shipped orders cannot be cancelled","The order lifecycle states (Placed, Invoiced, Shipped) are clear"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround that preserves invoice irreversibility","Whether Operations could delay invoicing to create a cancellation window","Whether consumer protection regulations mandate a cancellation right regardless of internal invoicing policy"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means the invoice is generated synchronously and immediately — there is no gap","The PM's requirement reflects a genuine customer need, not just a nice-to-have","All three stakeholders have equal authority to set requirements"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The PM requires cancellation before shipment. Finance forbids cancellation of invoiced orders. Operations invoices at placement. Combined: every order is invoiced the moment it is placed, so the PM's cancellation window is zero — cancellation is impossible. These three explicit requirements cannot all be satisfied simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Manager","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A) Decouple invoicing from placement — invoice at shipment instead, creating a cancellation window. Requires Operations to accept pricing risk.","B) Keep immediate invoicing but allow cancellation via credit notes — the invoice is never reversed (satisfying Finance), but a compensating credit note effectively cancels the order. Requires Finance to accept credit note workflow.","C) Keep immediate invoicing, block cancellation entirely — customers cannot cancel. PM must withdraw the requirement. High customer impact risk.","D) Introduce a 'hold' period — delay invoicing by N minutes/hours after placement, allowing cancellation in that window. Compromise between all parties."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the most common industry resolution. The invoice remains irreversible (Finance is satisfied), pricing is locked at placement (Operations is satisfied), and customers can cancel before shipment (PM is satisfied). The credit note is a standard accounting mechanism for this exact scenario. Recommend convening all three stakeholders to confirm."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement order cancellation as specified — the three requirements form a logical impossibility.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 (PM: cancel before shipment) is incompatible with BR-5 (Finance+Ops: invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled). Implementation of either rule necessarily violates the other."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature where three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided conflicting requirements about when an order may be cancelled. The PM allows cancellation before shipment, but Finance forbids cancellation of invoiced orders, and Operations invoices at placement — meaning orders are invoiced immediately and can never be cancelled under the Finance rule, directly contradicting the PM's requirement.","bounded_context":"Order Li

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

We have three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- PM says cancellation is allowed before shipping
- Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled
- Operations says orders are invoiced at placement time

So combining Finance + Operations: orders cannot be cancelled after placement (since they're invoiced immediately). This directly contradicts the PM's requirement that orders can be cancelled any time before shipping.


This is a fundamental conflict that needs to be resolved before we can move forward.
